justification: All standard ADR sections present (Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Considered). Crash-recovery lock behavior explicitly described in Consequences ('If the coordinator node crashes, PostgreSQL releases the lock when the connection drops'). All three alternatives listed (Redis SETNX, ZooKeeper, heartbeat table) with clear rejection rationale. Context explains the multi-node expansion problem accurately. No invented information — all content traces back to the scenario's MJ analysis. ADR is fully readable as a standalone document.

observations.text: Exceptionally well-structured ADR with clear separation of Rationale, Trade-offs, and Consequences — goes beyond minimum requirements
Crash-recovery risk covered in both Trade-offs ('No TTL' stall scenario) and Consequences (connection-drop release), with concrete mitigation
All three alternatives presented in a comparison table with specific rejection rationale
Context section clearly explains the multi-node expansion problem and why coordination is needed
Implementation Notes section adds practical value without inventing information
PgBouncer compatibility caveat is a valuable operational detail that demonstrates deep understanding
The handoff_brief JSON structure preceding the ADR is not itself an ADR — the actual ADR follows after, which could confuse consumers expecting a single document format

trials.justification: All standard ADR sections present (Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Considered). Crash-recovery lock behavior explicitly described in Rationale ('database connection drops and the session-level lock is automatically released') and further elaborated in Trade-offs Accepted (no TTL-based expiration risk with mitigation). All three alternatives listed: Redis SETNX, ZooKeeper, and custom heartbeat table. Context clearly explains the multi-node expansion problem. No invented information. ADR is fully readable as a standalone document.

observations.text: All six standard ADR sections present with clear structure and formatting
Crash-recovery behavior explicitly covered: session-level lock auto-releases on connection drop
All three MJ alternatives listed with concise rejection rationale in a well-formatted table
Context section clearly explains the multi-node expansion problem and why coordination is needed
Trade-offs Accepted section goes beyond minimum requirements, covering hang scenarios, observability gaps, and session-scoping risks with mitigations
No invented alternatives or fabricated context — stays faithful to source material
Includes practical operational guidance (runbooks, monitoring queries, timeout settings) that makes the ADR actionable

observations.text: Crash-recovery semantics clearly explained: session-level locks released when connection drops
All three alternatives from MJ listed in a well-structured comparison table with rejection rationale

Consequences split into Positive and Negative sections with actionable risk mitigations
Goes beyond minimum requirements with useful operational details (no-TTL hang scenario, connection pooler warning, lock key documentation) without inventing information
Status field present and set to Accepted
Handoff brief includes well-formed acceptance criteria with Given/When/Then structure
